activities_projects.title: Mapping Colonial Deforestation Drivers
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the specific economic and imperial demands that led to forest loss
steps: Identify the specific requirements of the British Royal Navy for timber and how it led to search parties in India.
Calculate the estimated number of trees required to provide sleepers for a 700-mile railway stretch.
Explain the expansion of cultivation as a colonial strategy to increase state revenue.
Diagram the link between the 'disappearing' oak forests in England and the timber exploitation in India.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how global imperial needs dictated local environmental changes.
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of historical data and logical connection of drivers (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Dietrich Brandis and Scientific Forestry
activities_projects.objective: Critically evaluate the colonial management of forests
materials: Reference material on the Imperial Forest Service
steps: Research the reforms introduced by Dietrich Brandis in 1864 and 1906.
Compare 'Natural Forests' with 'Scientific Forestry' (Plantations).
Explain the legal restrictions placed on local villagers regarding the use of timber and grazing.
Discuss the impact of the 1878 Forest Act categorizing forests into 'Reserved, Protected, and Village' forests.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the transition from community-based forest use to state-controlled commercial forestry.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of comparison between natural and managed forest systems (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Impact on Tribal Lives
activities_projects.objective: Understand the socio-economic consequences of forest laws on forest dwellers
steps: Identify the various ways villagers used forests for roots, leaves, fruits, and medicines.
Contrast this with the colonial view of forests as a source of timber for ships and railways.
Analyze why hunting, which was a traditional lifestyle, became 'illegal' for locals but a 'sport' for the British.
Write a short dialogue between a forest official and a villager caught collecting wood.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the criminalization of traditional livelihoods under colonial rule.
activities_projects.evaluation: Empathy and historical accuracy in the role-play/dialogue (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Shifting Cultivation: The Colonial Conflict
activities_projects.objective: Analyze why Swidden agriculture was viewed as a threat by the British
steps: Illustrate the process of 'Shifting Cultivation' (clearing, burning, sowing, and fallowing).
List the European arguments against this practice (e.g., loss of timber, danger of fire, difficulty in tax collection).
Explain how the ban on this practice forced communities into new, often exploitative, occupations.
Identify the different local names for shifting cultivation in Asia, Africa, and South America.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the conflict between traditional knowledge and modern colonial science.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the cultivation diagram and depth of colonial critique (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Bastar Rebellion (1910)
activities_projects.objective: Study a specific case of resistance against forest reservation
steps: Identify the key leaders like Gunda Dhur and the role of the Dhurwas of Kanger forest.
Detail the specific triggers for the 1910 rebellion (reservations, increased land rent).
Describe the methods of communication used by rebels (e.g., mango boughs, lumps of earth).
Discuss the outcomes of the rebellion and whether the people achieved their goals.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the effectiveness and symbolism of local tribal resistance.
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the research on rebellion events and outcomes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: New Trades, New Employments
activities_projects.objective: Examine the shift from forest use to industrial labor
steps: Identify how some communities benefited from new trades (e.g., Baigas, Koravas, Yerukula).
Contrast this with those forced to work in tea plantations in Assam.
Discuss the working conditions and wages in these colonial-run plantations.
Explain how the forest laws led to the 'sedentarization' of nomadic tribes.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the structural changes in labor caused by forest dispossession.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clear comparison of different economic paths taken by tribes (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Colonialism in Java: The Dutch System
activities_projects.objective: Compare Indonesian forest management with the Indian model
steps: Research the Dutch 'Blandongdiensten' system and its requirements.
Analyze the 'Samin's Challenge'—how Surontiko Samin led a non-violent protest against forest laws.
Compare the role of the 'Kalangs' of Java with the forest communities of India.
Discuss the 'Scorched Earth' policy used by the Dutch during World War II.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies universal patterns in colonial forest exploitation across different geographies.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of comparison between Dutch and British methods (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: War and Deforestation
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the impact of World War I and II on forest resources
steps: Explain how the 'War Needs' led to the abandonment of working plans in India.
Identify why the British cut trees freely during the two World Wars.
Discuss the long-term ecological impact of these 'emergency' timber extractions.
Compare this to the 'Scorched Earth' policy in Indonesia.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates global military conflicts to local environmental destruction.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in explaining the war-forestry link (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Evolution of Forest Laws
activities_projects.objective: Track the legislative changes in forest management from 1865 to 1927
steps: List the key features of the Forest Acts of 1865, 1878, and 1927.
Analyze why the 1878 Act was considered a turning point for rural communities.
Explain how these laws redefined 'theft' and 'crime' in the forest.
Describe the powers given to forest guards and their impact on daily life.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how law can be used as a tool for economic dispossession.
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical breakdown of legislative evolution and its impacts (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Modern Perspectives: Conservation vs. Rights
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate current trends in forest management compared to the colonial era
steps: Identify how modern conservation goals differ from colonial 'scientific forestry'.
Research the role of local communities in modern 'Sacred Groves' (Sarnas, Kan, Rai).
Explain the significance of the Forest Rights Act (2006) in contemporary India.
Discuss if the legacy of colonial forest laws still exists in modern forest management.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes historical knowledge to understand current environmental policy debates.
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical analysis of historical continuity and change (10 marks)
